coredata.dc:title: A novel metronidazole fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots embedded silica molecularly imprinted polymer
coredata.dc:creator: Mehrzad-Samarin, Mina
Faridbod, Farnoush
Dezfuli, Amin Shiralizadeh
Ganjali, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: A novel optical nanosensor for detection of Metronidazole in biological samples was reported. Graphene quantum dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (GQDs-embedded SMIP) was synthesized and used as a selective fluorescent probe for Metronidazole detection. The new synthesized GQDs-embedded SMIP showed strong fluorescent emission at 450nm excited at 365nm which quenched in presence of Metronidazole as a template molecule.. The quenching was proportional to the concentration of Metronidazole in a linear range of at least 0.2μM to 15μM. The limit of detection for metronidazole determination was obtained 0.15μM. The nanosensor successfully worked in plasma matrixes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanosensor
Fluorescence detection
Graphene quantum dots
Metronidazole
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Inorganic silica polymer

Sample preparation of the human plasma
Preparation and characterization of the GQDs-embedded SMIP
Absorption and photoluminescence properties of GQDs-embedded SMIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP (blue curve), FT-IR spectra of silica-GQDs (red curve).
(a) SEM image of GQDs-embedded SMIP; (b) TEM image of GQDs -embedded SMIP (c) TEM image of GQD.
(a) UV–Vis spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP, (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP.

Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-embedded SNIP, GQDs-embedded SMIP before and after template molecules extraction, GQDs-embedded SMIP with addition of 20μM of MNZ. The concentration of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP was 2mgmL−1.

Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP with addition of various amount of 20μM of MNZ in PBS buffer (10mM, pH=9) and related Stern-Volmer plots from GQDs-embedded SMIP and SNIP with MNZ. The concentration of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs -embedded SNIP was 2.5mgmL−1.

(a) Selective adsorption of MNZ, ONZ, DMZ and CAM (2μM) by GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP at pH 9.5 (PBS buffer, 10mM), the concentration of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP was 2.5mgmL−1; (b) Influence of coexisting common ions (10µM) on the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of GQDs-embedded SMIP in the presence of MNZ (5µM).
Illustration of the preparation of GQDs -embedded SMIP. .
Results of the analysis of plasma samples.

head.ce:title: A novel metronidazole fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots embedded silica molecularly imprinted polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel optical nanosensor for detection of Metronidazole in biological samples was reported. Graphene quantum dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (GQDs-embedded SMIP) was synthesized and used as a selective fluorescent probe for Metronidazole detection. The new synthesized GQDs-embedded SMIP showed strong fluorescent emission at 450nm excited at 365nm which quenched in presence of Metronidazole as a template molecule.. The quenching was proportional to the concentration of Metronidazole in a linear range of at least 0.2μM to 15μM. The limit of detection for metronidazole determination was obtained 0.15μM. The nanosensor successfully worked in plasma matrixes.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have been recently found a significant place in designing chemical sensors/biosensors (Meixner et al., 2008; Honeychurch, 2014). Fluorescent nanomaterials are considered as a good candidate for using in designing optical sensing systems (Kneipp et al., 2010). Quantum dots are one of the fluorescent nanomaterials which are newly applied in designing sensors/biosensors for the high sensitive determination of some analytes (Xu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2015; Hao et al., 2016). Their unique optical properties, such as high optical quantum yield, narrow and symmetrical spectra, broad excitation spectra and photo-stability make them appropriate for this purpose (Zhou et al., 2017).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are respectable choice among the quantum dots because of remarkable advantages, such as low toxicity, high fluorescent emission, robust chemical inertness, and excellent photo-stability (Hou et al., 2016). GQDs are used with different strategies in designing nanosensors. However, one of the approaches to improve the selectivity of QDs based nanosensors is using molecular imprinted polymer (MIP) technology. MIP is a kind of biomimetic polymers, which is usually synthesized by the polymerization of the functional and cross-linking monomers in the presence of a template molecule capable of forming complexes with the monomer. After removal of the template, definite cavities, specific to both shape and chemical functionality of the template are remained. Thus, MIP can recognize the template molecule with a high specificity like enzyme-substrate interaction (Wei et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2016). GQDs embedded in MIP can offer the advantages of high sensitivity of fluorescent emission coupled with high selectivity of MIP, to design sensitive and selective nanosensors. When target molecules (the analytes) place in MIP cavities, the fluorescence emission of the quantum dots changes (Ren and Chen, 2015). This can be useful to determination of the target analyte. Literature survey reveals few reports on this new strategy of sensing based nanomaterials (Hao et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2014).

Molecular imprinting can be performed in organic and inorganic polymers. Sol–gel processing is the most common strategy for synthesis of inorganic ones. One of the advantage of this method is using of eco-friendly reaction solvent (water or ethanol), which is quite different from the general solvent (chloroform, acetonitrile or toluene) (Lofgreen and Ozin, 2014). Several fluorescent nanosensors based QDs and MIP have been recently reported for some analytes including carbon dots for sensing tetracycline (Hou et al., 2016), Mn-doped ZnS QDs for detection of cyphenothrin (Ren and Chen, 2015), and graphene quantum dots and carbon dot embedded MIP for determination of paranitrophenol (Zhou et al., 2014; Hao et al., 2016).

In this work, a novel highly selective and sensitive optical nanosensor based on GQDs-embedded silica molecularly imprinted polymer (SMIP) is introduced for the first time for a none-fluorescent pharmaceutical compound detection, Metronidazole (MNZ), in plasma matrix.

Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole-based drug, used as antibacterial and antiprotozoa medication. It is generally arranged in the treatment of anti-anaerobic infections, follicle worms, and acne diseases. Long-term using this drug leads to drug resistance, which cause a posing health hazards to the consumers. However, due to its low price and high efficacy in the treatment of bacteria, MNZ is still illegally used as an additive in the cosmetic materials by many commercial companies (Liu et al., 2015). When the MNZ accumulates in human body, some toxic effects including seizures and peripheral neuropathy, can be occurred which is harmful to the health of both humans and wildlife (Xiao et al., 2016). Thus, its determination can be of great importance.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Citric acid, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS), methanol, ethanol and other chemical reagents were obtained from Merck Chem. Co (Germany). PBS buffer (pH=9) was used during the experiments. Metronidazole (MNZ) and other standards were purchased from local company (Iran). Double distilled water was used during the experiments.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic measurements were performed on Perkin-Elmer Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Model CM30 electron micro-scope (Philiphs). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using Model S4160 electron micro-scope (Hitachi). The fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Perkin-Elmer LS50 luminescence spectrometer.

GQDs were prepared by pyrolyzing citric acid as described elsewhere (Dong et al., 2012). Briefly, 2g of citric acid was put into a 5mL beaker and heated to 200°C for about 30min, until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, the liquid was dissolved into 100mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution drop by drop, with continuous stirring. The obtained GQD solution was adjusted to pH=8 with 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution and stored in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs-embedded SMIP and SNIP
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 5mmol APTS was added into 16mL GQDs solution. The mixture was stirred and heated up to 40°C for 30min. After natural cooling to room temperature, the APTS-modified GQDs (GQDs-APTS) were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether twice, and then dispersed in 40mL anhydrous ethanol for further experiments.

The SMIPs were fabricated on the silica-embedded GQDs via a typical sol-gel polymerization. In a 50mL beaker, 100mg MNZ (template) was added to 10mL GQDs-APTS ethanol solution under stirring. Then, 2mL of APTS and 1mL TEOS were added to the solution, respectively. Subsequently, 0.5mL of the catalytic solution containing 2g NH4F, 100mLH2O and 23mL NH4OH were added to the reaction beaker and the mixture was stirred for 30min.

The final products were collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with anhydrous ethanol. The original MNZ template molecules in the composites were extracted with methanol and 10% acidified methanol, until no MNZ could be detected in the washing solvent by UV spectroscopy. The composites were dried at 60°C overnight to eliminate the solvent completely. Finally, the GQDs-embedded SMIP were dispersed in 40mL water and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C prior to use. As a control, the non-imprinted polymers capped GQDs (GQDs-embedded SNIP) were synthesized in the same manner without the addition of template molecules (MNZ).

The recognition capacity of GQDs-embedded SMIP was measured with different MNZ concentrations by fluorescence spectroscopy, and the fluorescent intensity of the maximum emission peak 450nm was used for quantitative analysis. The fluorescence measurements were carried out by fitting the excitation wavelength at 365nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission were both 15nm. A quartz cell 4.5mL with 10mm width was used.

The stock solution of GQDs-embedded SMIP was diluted with PBS buffer solution (pH=9). Then, 100μL above mixture and different amounts of MNZ standard solution (20µM) were sequentially injected into the cuvette. The final concentration of MNZ can be known through a simple calculation. All the solutions were shaken thoroughly before measurement. The controlled experiments of GQDs-embedded SNIP were the same as those of GQDs-embedded SMIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation of the human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Drug free human plasma was obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Service (Tehran, Iran) and stored at −20°C until use after gentle thawing. In order to precipitate the plasma proteins, the plasma samples were treated with 20μl nitric acid 1molL−1. After that, the mixture was vortexed for a 30s and then centrifuged at 6000rpm for 10min. Then 100μL aliquot of the obtained supernatant was diluted 100 times and used for analyzing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the GQDs-embedded SMIP
ce:section.ce:para: With the purpose of improve the interaction between GQDs and molecularly imprinted silica matrix, GQDs have to be embedded with a layer of silica before the sol–gel hydrolysis and condensation imprinted polymerization reaction (Mao et al., 2012). Synthesis of GQDs-embedded SMIP consists of three steps. First one is synthesize of GQDs, the second step is synthesize GQDs-APTS as fluorescent materials, and next, the GQDs-embedded SMIP were prepared via a simple sol-gel polymerization in the presence of GQDs-APTS as fluorescent materials, MNZ as template, APTS as functional monomer, TEOS as cross-linker and the catalytic solution as mentioned above. After the removal of template the GQDs-embedded SMIP were fabricated. Synthesis of GQDs-embedded SMIP is illustrated in Scheme 1.

As shown in Fig. 1, the FT-IR absorption spectrum of GQDs-embedded SMIP (blue curve), the strong and broad peaks around 1072cm−1 and 1120cm−1 are assigned to the Si-O-C and Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching (Li et al., 2010), respectively show that silica attached GQDs. Peaks at about 760cm−1 and 460cm−1 also suggest the existence of the Si-O vibrations. Additionally, the broad absorption peak around 3440cm−1 (N-H bond) and 2900cm−1 (C-H stretching band) are attributed to the aminopropyl groups (Zhou et al., 2014). The peak at 1650cm−1 demonstrates the presence of amide bonding (-CO-NH-) (Mao et al., 2012). As shown in the FT-IR absorption spectrum of SMIP-embedded GQDs, peaks at Si–O–Si (at 1120cm−1) and Si–O–C (at 1072cm−1) become stronger compared with that of silica-GQDs (red curve). These results confirm the copolymerization of TEOS and APTS on the surface of silica-embedded GQDs.

The morphology of GQDs-embedded SMIP which was characterized by SEM and TEM were shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b). The GQDs-embedded SMIP particles exhibited size distributions with around 100nm diameters. The TEM of GQD is shown in Fig. 2(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and photoluminescence properties of GQDs-embedded SMIP
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared GQDs-embedded SMIP show a wide range of UV–Vis adsorption band from 300nm to 400nm, it was corresponding to the aromatic π system and band gap and absorption edge of the GQDs. When excited at 365nm, the GQDs-embedded SMIP show asymmetric fluorescence emission peak at 450nm in ( Fig. 3). These results show that the silica coating is optically transparent and inert and not limited the PL properties of GQDs (Sun et al., 2007). The fluorescent GQDs-embedded SMIP can be used to efficiently MNZ sensing. As shown in Fig. 4, after template removal, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-embedded SMIP increased. Also, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-embedded SMIP quenched greatly when interacted with MNZ molecules, and an energy transfer between GQDs and MNZ molecules was recommended to be responsible for the fluorescence quenching of the GQDs-embedded SMIP (Han et al., 2009). Therefore, the fluorescent emission of GQDs-embedded SMIP efficiently decrease in presence of MNZ molecules.

The pH effect on the fluorescence ratio (F0/F−1) of GQDs-embedded SMIP was investigated by tuning pH from 3.0 to 11.0. Herein, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the composite before and after the addition of MNZ, respectively. The fluorescence quenching of the sensor by MNZ increases with the pH increasing from 3.0 to 9.0, and then decreases slightly from pH 9.0–11.0. Thus, pH 9.0 was chosen as the optimum pH in the detection of MNZ solution in this work. In acidic pH the heteroatoms of MNZ or even binding site were protonated and causes a decrease in interaction of template molecule. Also, in acidic solution maximum absorbance of the MNZ molecule shifts to the lower wave lengths (Moffat et al., 2003) and causes decreasing in photoinduced electron transfer between GQDs and MNZ.

The results indicates a stable emission property of GQDs-embedded SMIP within 60s. When MNZ molecules rebind to the binding sites of GQDs-embedded SMIP, the energy transfer from GQDs-embedded SMIP to MNZ makes the exciting energy low and then the emission will decreases.

The quenching of the fluorescence can be attributed to a kind of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) (Lackowicz, 2006). PET is an excited state electron transfer process by which the excited electron is transferred from donor (here GQDs) to acceptor (here MNZ). When a photon excites a molecule, an electron in a ground state orbital can be excited to a higher energy orbital. This excited state leaves a vacancy in a ground state orbital that can be filled by an electron donor. It produces an electron in a high energy orbital which can be donated to an electron acceptor. In these respects a photoexcited molecule can act as a good oxidizing agent or a good reducing agent. The end result of both reactions is that an electron is delivered to an orbital that is higher in energy than where it previously resided. This is often described as a charge separated electron-hole pair when working with semiconductors.
In the absence of a proper electron donor or acceptor it is possible for such molecules to undergo ordinary fluorescence emission.

The –NH2 groups at the surface of GQDs-embedded SMIP and SNIP acted as the binding sites to combine MNZ through hydrogen bond interactions (Hou et al., 2016). The fluorescent intensities of GQDs-embedded SMIP were measured after equilibrium in PBS buffer (pH=9) and plotted against the MNZ concentrations ranging from 0 to 20μmolL−1 ( Fig. 5). Although both GQDs-embedded SMIP and SNIP showed fluorescence quenching response to MNZ, the fluorescent intensity of GQDs-embedded SMIP was quenched remarkably with increasing the concentration of MNZ. The non-imprinted composites GQDs-embedded SNIP did not show obvious difference with the addition of MNZ. Compared with the SNIP-embedded ones, the GQDs-embedded SMIP have binding sites suitable for the attraction with template MNZ molecule. GQDs-embedded MIP had been more sensitive for the MNZ determination. The fluorescence quenching performance can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
where F0 and F represent the fluorescent intensities at a given MNZ concentration and in a MNZ free solution, respectively, Q is the concentration of MNZ, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant of the target molecule (Hou et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 5, the Stern-Volmer plots of GQDs-embedded SMIP and SNIP-embedded GQDs both exhibited linear relationship by changing the concentration of MNZ from 20nM to 15μM. The regression equations were 0.995 and 0.986 for SMIP and SNIP respectively.

with increasing the initial concentration of MNZ, the adsorption capacity of GQDs-embedded SMIP and SNIP increased rapidly. However, the amount of MNZ bound to GQDs-embedded SMIP was higher than that bound to GQDs-embedded SNIP, because of the efficiency of molecularly imprinting. One of the most important properties of sensors application is reproducibility. The GQDs-embedded SMIP sensor could be reused for at least 5 repeated analyses. Therefore, the proposed sensor could be applied to the quantitative analysis of trace MNZ. The linear range of the calibration curve was obtained from 0.2 to 15μmolL−1. As shown in Fig. 5, the detection limit, calculated based on a 3σ (Ivanova and Spiteller, 2012), was down to 151.3nmolL−1. The repeatability and reproducibility of the proposed nanosensor were also satisfactory (RSD 4.4% and 5.2%, respectively). The method reproducibility was examined by determining the precisions. The precision was evaluated by measuring the MNZ three times; the precision was carried out over 3 days by analyzing the MNZ. The relative standard deviations (RSD), 4.4% was obtained.

The GQDs-embedded SMIP fluorescent nanosensor was used for selective and sensitive detection of MNZ in biological fluids such as plasma samples. The recovery was evaluated by comparing the analytic signals of MNZ obtained from the spiked plasma with those of the same concentration standard solution (n=4, average of four replicate measurements). The results shown in Table 1, Indicated that the proposed method provided wide dynamic range, good accuracy and a lower detection limit. Therefore, the GQDs-embedded SMIP fluorescent sensor is more proper and capable for the on-site and rapid MNZ analysis in complex samples.

The selectivity of the proposed nanosensor was investigated using three compounds having the chemical structure analogue or close to the template MNZ, including Ornidazole (ONZ) and Dimetridazole (DMZ) ( Fig. 6(a)). In the synthesis process, many specific recognition sites with respect to the template were generated on the surface of MIPs, and these sites were complementary in shape, size, and spatial arrangement to MNZ, thus, MNZ molecules had superiority in occupying the binding sites over the other analogs. The template MNZ could be bound strongly to the imprinted site and cause significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. For NIPs fluorescence quenching of all the analogs and MNZ was quite close and very little, suggesting that the NIPs could not effectively bind MNZ as well as its analogs (Fig. 6(b)).

To address the question whether foreign coexisting substances show effect on the analysis of MNZ or not, further interference experiments were performed in the presence of MNZ together with other co-existing ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, NO3 −, Cl−). As shown in Fig. 6(b) there is no obvious effect on the assaying MNZ. Because of the specific spatial effect, other coexisting substances cannot be bound to the recognition sites, which prove the high selectivity for target analytes. As a result, the proposed GQDs-embedded SMIP based sensor exhibited more accessible sites for the absorption of MNZ and high selectivity without cross-reactivity.

The combination of fluorescence detection and imprinting technique not only made the sensor applicable to detect MNZ molecules directly, but also improved the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor. The as prepared GQDs-embedded SMIP composites were applied to the trace detection of MNZ in plasma samples successfully. This eco-friendly optical sensor demonstrated a lower detection limit and good accuracy in complex samples analysis, which reveals great potential in advanced analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Research Council of University of Tehran (6102027) for the financial support of this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanosensors for Chemical and Biological Applications Sensing with Nanotubes
Sensors, Micro-and Nanosensor Technology: Trends in Sensor Markets
Clarke's Analysis of Drugs and Poisons
